​@TheGrooveMoose  You're going with a presupposition because that's what you already believe. In what part of this universe, in which you have lived your whole life, have you ever seen evidence that even one other exists, let alone multiple?
Your presupposition requires scientific analysis that cannot possibly be done within the next millenia or, I would suggest, ever; ironically an appeal to science with no scientific basis or evidence. Even the supposition that unless something can be measured, quantified and documented, it does not exist, proves itself objectively false because such a presupposition cannot be quantified, measured or documented. Using an immaterial statement, you deny the existence of the immaterial. My supposition requires simple, logical deduction with observable reality as its foundation. You require far more faith than I do.

replies.author: @TheGrooveMoose
replies.text: ​@silas6328Anyway, in which other universe have you lived for comparison?

replies.author: @silas6328
replies.text:
​@TheGrooveMoose really? Your response is to ask a rhetorical question again? Fine. I acknowledge the possibility that other universes might exist, despite there being no evidence whatsoever. I also acknowledge the possibility that mermaids and the Loch Ness Monster might exist and I consider that possibility more likely, given documented reports of sitings, as does demonic activity, for that matter. And, assuming there are other universes, they ultimately answer nothing and no significant questions about the origin of existence for one simple reason: where did THEY come from? Occam's Razor sides with me once again as the purely naturalistic assumption that there is no God only kicks the can down the road.
But, I guess it's POSSIBLE that once we figure out how to prove the existence of the alternate universes that we have no evidence for, we can then figure out how to go to those universes and learn what universes created THEM and on and on for the next thirty trillion years of scientific exploration.
Or maybe an intelligent being that is infinite both in breadth and in power, whose existence, if not necessarily his full nature, can be proven by logical process, created something from nothing.

replies.text: ​@silas6328The reason I ask is because you've come to the "logical" conclusion that this universe and everything in it could not exist without a creator without even knowing what a universe without a creator would look like for comparison, which makes your "logical" conclusion confusing.

replies.author: @silas6328
replies.text: ​@TheGrooveMoose it's logical because entropy is a thing. We have observed its effects and we know it to be true. The reason our universe has to have been created by something is because it cannot be infinite. Its own natural laws make that impossible. Whoever or whatever created it must then be infinite, or else you have to explain where IT came from as well. This is the most fundamental question of existence: why is there something instead of nothing? Your theory either answers that question or your theory, logically, is wrong.

replies.author: @AnonymousNoNayme
replies.text:
@dvof5198 Right, but it is and always was a part of (the lands belonging/connected to) China and he didn't specify China "the concept/country" so saying "China" is only technically incorrect. If you don't care about that distinction it is irrelevant and still "China" in the general location sense. 

Your assumption is only right if he doesn't know better, but if he does know better, doesn't care, and was just speaking in deliberately general terms, then it is you who misunderstood his intention (likely due to pedantry).

There isn't enough info to know for sure what he meant exactly. It's entirely feasible he is referring to "China" in the colloquial sense of "the lands where the majority of the Chinese peoples reside" which would include Hong Kong regardless of who owns it.

In that sense/framing it is both "part of China" (it's connected to it and is full of Chinese people) and "China" (lands where the Chinese people live).

No Brits went to Hong Kong, gestured to the hoard of Chinese people and went "look at all these fine upstanding british folk!"

When people traveled back then and someone asked them where they were going, they said "China", followed by "oh whereabouts?", "Hong Kong!", "Ah, staying in the colonies then old chap?", "Indubitably!" "Pip pip, Cheerio!"

Hong Kong was both "not in China" (proper) and "over in China" (in the general/colloquial sense).

Hopefully the english lesson has helped 👍

Not to forget, had it been sent to China, they may never have got it back due to relations between the West and China not being as cordial as they perhaps are today.

replies.text:
@BlackTortoiseOfTheNorth One of the pilots hired for aerial shots was about to do a test flight when he hit a swarm of birds, forcing him to emergency land on a road. He hit only one car: his wife's, with their kid on board, killing both.

One of the zookeepers got killed by the big cats the night before they were supposed to shoot another zoo scene with said cats.

When one of the producers flew to England, his plane was hit by lightning (not a big deal, happens all the time and planes are built to withstand that). Two weeks later the same happened to the writer's plane. Quite the coincidence.

When they tested the effect of the pole falling off the church and impaling a characters, one the producers got cocky and stood where the character was supposed to stand in the shot. The pole narrowly missed him.

author: @cynthiajohnson9412
text: I remember when I was in high school and I read the 'Amityville Horror' and then stuff started happening in our old house like once I found  dozens and dozens of flies around a window in the wing of the house I slept in and I'm like where did these flies come from - there's no rotting food or dead animals or anything that would hatch maggots. Very strange.  Then the ceiling above my bed began to leak. Our house had a metal roof and my dad couldn't figure out where the water was coming from. Then one night 50 lbs of plaster (I weighted the plaster chunks as I was cleaning up the mess) came crashing down onto my bed. Luckily I was lying on the outer edge of the bed and was not crushed. Another night I thought I heard hushed unintelligible male voices whispering in the empty air above my bed. I just closed my eyes really tight, recited the Lord's prayer and chalked it up to imagination. But I nailed a little silver cross above the door of my bedroom. And now older and wiser, I've concluded there are somethings you just don't mess with, don't invite them in, cause whether you 'believe' or not, once in they might be problematic is getting them out again. And while I'm not strictly what anyone would call 'religious' I do ask angels to protect me. And some books and movies I just leave be and keep walking.  My spiritual views on this kind of stuff is soft, but I do firmly believe you do have to invite stuff in. So better safe than sorry, is my current philosophy. That's my philosophy for everything now, you become, and your life reflects, the stuff you invite in, so choose carefully. It's all fun and games until....it's not.  P.S. One of the reasons why I have no sympathy for all the people demanding love, care, concern, and attention from society at large these days, is because when I told my parents that the ceiling had just come crashing down on to my bed, they were annoyed, and told me to clean up the mess and go back to sleep. So the cries of the 'unloved' leave me  rather unmoved.

replies.author: @PerkulatorBenny
replies.text: Whether you personally believe or not, I find it's better to treat these things like a Pascal's wager: You have nothing to lose by not messing around even if they aren't real, and everything to gain (or avoid) if they are.

replies.author: @cynthiajohnson9412
replies.text: @PerkulatorBenny Gain? As in selling your soul to the devil gain?

replies.author: @justforever96
replies.text: All of those things are just normal for an old house. The flies cluster inside the walls and crowd to the window because of the light, and die in hundreds sometimes. It's typical to find dead flies under the windows. Leaking roofs happen and can be notorious for figuring out where the actual leak is if the water doesn't just straight down, which is usually doesn't. And if your roof is leaking into your room it makes sense that the plaster would fail at some point. It's not supposed to get wet. Don't think any of that was because your house was haunted.

replies.author: @PerkulatorBenny
replies.text:
@cynthiajohnson9412 No, more like the "gain" is avoiding trouble if hauntings turn out to be real, because you didn't mess around.
I.e. If you take it seriously and don't mess with anything that could lead to being haunted, then if hauntings aren't real nothing happens anyway (so you lose nothing because there was never anything to lose). But if they are real, you avoid trouble because you didn't mess around (you "gain everything" because the only positive to be had was not getting into trouble, and you didn't invite any trouble).

replies.author: @cynthiajohnson9412
replies.text: @PerkulatorBenny That logic works for me. It also works the same if you are somehow creating with your mind the phenom.  as well, as opposed to it exists as separate entities. I strongly suspect that we have mental powers of conjuring up stuff that do somehow manifest in the physical world. Don't quote me on that. But I've 'manifested' some uncanny good stuff in my life, I wouldn't be shocked to find out you can manifest bad stuff. I also like to watch 'Celebrity Ghost Stories' and a surprising number of segments concern people either living in a place where some previous resident 'invited' stuff in or they were messing with a ouija board or were having a seance or went to a grave yard and invited something in.  Just be careful what kind of doors you open. I didn't open any doors in my old house, but I did open my mind to the existences of that stuff by bringing the book in. When I was really little I prayed really, really hard that I would never 'see' anything. I've never really seen anything that would scare be, at least when I'm awake. So I kept that door firmly closed. Later I opened myself up and asked to see something, but nothing that would scare me. So I might have seen a ghost, or I might have just seen a weird old man somewhere that didn't make sense for him to be, who didn't apparently see me even thought I was standing right in front of him waving my arms like an idiot - he looked right through me. If that was actually something supernatural, I did open the door to that. But it didn't scare me, it was just very strange.

replies.author: @RobertQualkinbush
replies.text: When being abducted by an “alien” the person prayed, “Jesus help me!” And it stopped. Word to the wise.

replies.author: @cynthiajohnson9412
replies.text: @RobertQualkinbush Interesting. I myself have always been in search of what I call a unified theory on all phenom. so I'm always trying to both think and feel/intuit my way through everything. But as for aliens, as I've said in the past, I have no basis for an opinion on that. I had a friend years ago who wanted me to listen to these series of tapes he had on aliens - it was something like three hours. So listened, I like to keep an open mind. It seemed for the first 2 and a half hours to be just an exploration and then it got into some weird stuff about warring alien races. So I gave the tapes back to my friend and he asked what I thought. I said I have no basis for an opinion, but between you and me I hope not. My definition of 'belief' is very different from that most people and I don't think they even have a standard definition. The only things I really believe in are gravity and the laws of physics. Meaning I don't step in front of moving vehicles as objects don't come to an instantaneous stop. Pretty much everything else I evaluated on a helpful/unhelpful basis. Or as in it's unhelpful to my mental state  to believe in violent aliens.  So until I need to, since there's nothing I can do about them anyway, I'll just put a pin in that.  And prayers for protection seems to work. But at the end of the day we can't even begin to understand what forces are at work in the universe, so the pragmatic approach would be to be optimistic, do what you can with what you have, don't attach to firmly to beliefs unless they are helpful and improve your mental state and the state of your relationships in the world. We all need reasons to keep going. And try to be the best person you can, the kind of person you hope others will be. But mostly steer away from the dark and ugly, steer your kids away as well and trust there is someone up there who likes us and wants the best for us, and just keep plugging. I myself am having one of the all time worst years of my life and February isn't even over yet. I was just saying that to my neighbor who helping me out for the 9th time this year alone and he, an old stoic Vermonter, was nodding yes you are having a hell of a horrible year - so it's not just my imagination or a chip on my shoulder for that. Anyway, stay away from the dark supernatural forces, given what we all are dealing with now, we don't need anymore trouble and have faith that everything will work out, even if we can't see how. If I didn't cling to that, I'd lose my freakin mind or become a miserable, insufferable wretch that I wouldn't blame God for abandoning. lol  P.S. My rule of thumb is that I try to stay open minded to ideas, until I get to darkness and violence. Some religions have some very dark and violent stuff that is both excused and advocated for. I say no to that. Meanwhile Jesus is the prince of peace and unconditional love. But he also turned over the tables of the money changers in the temple. So he did throw an elbow or two on occasion. I think we are in an age where we do have to fight back, we just don't want to get sucked into hating evil. Just walk the other way, protect yourself and your loved ones, but mostly don't get too close to evil. The same friend who gave me the tapes on aliens lent me a book on evil (he was a German Jew who twice escaped the Nazis, but his parents did not). The author did a deep dive into evil and even explored exorcism. His conclusion is that the chief danger of evil is that it seduces the good into hating it. So you don't want to deny evil, but you don't want to get too close either. Just turn toward the light and ask the Lord for protection.

replies.author: @cynthiajohnson9412
replies.text: @RobertQualkinbush If I had so say, I would say aliens as well as ghosts and God do exist. I've collected ghost stories from eye witnesses since I was a kid and I have too many to think there's nothing to them - full body apparition sightings from credible people even. My neighbors down the road, real no nonsense people, told of an old woman (identified by a picture of the previous deceased resident of their house) walked through their kitchen a couple of times.  And I think aliens probably have visited here for a very long time. Even Carl Sagan, an intellectual that I admire, said they probably existed. And if they wanted to they could have pretty much done whatever they wanted to us, and there isn't anything we could do about it. But since they haven't, I'd say they're probably looking out for us, likely better than we have have ourselves. As for demons, they probably exist as well but if you keep a pure, good heart you don't have to worry about them. But nature abhors a vacuum so being an empty vessel without any strength of character and attachment to the light might make some people susceptible to dark control. So like the Buddhists call their path the middle way, you don't want to get too strict in your belief in your own goodness and righteousness and wander into evil in pursuit of destroying it , but you don't want to be compass-less either. So play it by ear.

replies.author: @cynthiajohnson9412

author: @Komikino
text: When I was in high school (late 80s), I read a book on subliminal messages and how they are used in advertising, music and movies. There was a really huge chapter in the book that talked about this movie. The book freaked me out so much that I never wanted to see this movie... and probably never will.

replies.author: @neilmi80
replies.text: Please elaborate - was there extra creepy subliminal messaging in the film?

replies.author: @Komikino
replies.text:
@neilmi80 Oh man... this was WAY BACK in '85 or '86 when I read this so it's straining trying to remember things. 
Some stuff I remember is lighting and casting shadows. From what I read and remember, there are scenes where the girl doesn't cast a shadow on the walls like she should when the other actors have their shadows cast upon the same wall. 
There's a scene where the mother is in the attic (I think it was her because again, I haven't seen the movie), and she is using a candle to light up the darkness as she's looking for something. The candle erupts into a big flame and there is a sound of a cough that was distorted for the audio effect. In the book they said that when that scene happened, people would start coughing in the theater. 
There was also the sounds of pigs squealing (that was distorted or played backwards) in scenes to cause anxiety. I think they mentioned the sounds of bees being used as well.
I wish I could remember the name of this book....

replies.author: @Komikino
replies.text: @neilmi80 I just did some Google searches and couldn't find anything on the book since I can't recall the title. But I did find a Vanity Fair article where they talk about some of the scenes they used subliminal stuff in. Look up "Vanity Fair Exorcist subliminal 2023" and you should find it.

author: @jamesbednar3108
text:
Awesome behind-the-scenes information. Knew most of this stuff but learned new things and well. My Pastor at church is from the St Louis, Missouri, area where the actual event took place. He also took his Pastor-training there as well. We were talking about this movie once for whatever reason and he told me that whatever happened was pretty much true and the book/movie barely scratched the surface. The event was also part of his Pastor-training, so I'll believe him.

Have also visited the grave site of the actual preacher that performed the exorcism, he is also buried in the St Louis area. I had actual GPS coordinates to the gravesite but it took me about 15- minutes wandering around until found it for GPS was going bonkers, or I had an over active imagination that day. Eventually found the gravesite and it was just a plain marker.

Anyway, do remember watching this movie at the theater when it came out. For a 10-year old boy the audience reaction was way more entertaining than the movie. People fainting, popcorn flying through the air, people going down on their knees praying/begging for forgiveness, people running for the doors, and much more!! Oh, the memories!!
